content:
# John Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 — "On the third day" vs. "Two days later" for Greek τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And on the third day a wedding took place in Kana of the Galilaia" | Literal; transliterated place name |
| LOGOS | "On the third day there was a wedding at Cana in Galilee" | Formal; standard English |
| PNEUMA | "Two days later there was a wedding in the village of Cana in Galilee" | Dynamic; converts inclusive counting to modern reckoning; adds "village" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "On the third day." PNEUMA's "Two days later" modernizes the time reference but alters the text. PNEUMA also adds "village" which is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:3 — "wine having run short" vs. "When the wine ran out" — structural differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And wine having run short, the mother of the Yeshua says to him, 'They have no wine.'" | Genitive absolute; present tense "says" |
| LOGOS | "When the wine ran out, the mother of Jesus said to him, 'They have no wine.'" | Temporal clause; past tense |
| PNEUMA | "When the wine ran out, Jesus' mother said to him, 'They have no more wine.'" | Similar to LOGOS; adds "more" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "more" (not in Greek). EMET preserves the historical present tense, which is a Greek narrative convention.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:4 — "Woman" vs. "Dear woman" for Greek γύναι; "My hour" vs. "My time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What [is that] to me and to you, woman? The hour of me has not yet come." | Literal Semitic idiom; "woman"; "hour" |
| LOGOS | "Woman, what does this have to do with me? My hour has not yet come." | Formal; "Woman"; "hour" |
| PNEUMA | "'Dear woman, that's not our problem,' Jesus replied. 'My time has not yet come.'" | Adds "Dear"; paraphrases idiom as "that's not our problem"; "time" instead of "hour" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "Dear" softens what is a deliberately formal address. "That's not our problem" is colloquial paraphrase. "Time" instead of "hour" (ὥρα) loses the Johannine technical term — "the hour" is a recurring theme in John (7:30, 8:20, 12:23, 13:1, 17:1).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:6 — "two or three measures" vs. "twenty or thirty gallons" for Greek μετρητὰς δύο ἢ τρεῖς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "holding two or three measures each" | Literal; retains ancient measurement |
| LOGOS | "each holding twenty or thirty gallons" | Formal; converts to modern measurement |
| PNEUMA | "Each could hold twenty to thirty gallons." | Dynamic; same conversion as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — converting to gallons for accessibility. EMET's "measures" (μετρητής, approximately 10 gallons) requires a footnote to be meaningful.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:8 — "chief-of-the-feast" vs. "master of the feast" vs. "master of ceremonies" for Greek ἀρχιτρίκλινος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the chief-of-the-feast" | Literal compound |
| LOGOS | "the master of the feast" | Formal English |
| PNEUMA | "the master of ceremonies" | Modern equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "master of the feast." PNEUMA's "master of ceremonies" has modern connotations that don't fit an ancient wedding.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:10 — "when they have become drunk" vs. "when people have drunk freely" vs. "after everyone's had plenty to drink" for Greek ὅταν μεθυσθῶσιν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when they have become drunk, the inferior" | Literal; "become drunk" / "inferior" |
| LOGOS | "when people have drunk freely, then the poor wine" | Formal; euphemistic "drunk freely" |
| PNEUMA | "after everyone's had plenty to drink...the cheaper wine" | Dynamic; "plenty to drink" / "cheaper wine" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "when people have drunk freely" is the established rendering. EMET's "become drunk" is more literal (μεθυσθῶσιν does mean "become intoxicated") but stark. PNEUMA's paraphrase is colloquial.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 — "manifested his glory" vs. "revealed his glory" for Greek ἐφανέρωσεν τὴν δόξαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he revealed the glory of him" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "manifested his glory" | Formal; "manifested" |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus revealed his glory" | Dynamic; "revealed"; adds "Jesus" as subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "manifested his glory." Both "manifested" and "revealed" work; "manifested" has a slightly more formal register appropriate to the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "Passover/Pascha" and "Jews/Ioudaioi"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Pascha of the Ioudaioi...the Yeshua went up to Hierosolyma" | Transliterated terms throughout |
| LOGOS | "The Passover of the Jews was at hand, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem." | Standard English |
| PNEUMA | "It was nearly time for the Jewish Passover celebration, so Jesus traveled to Jerusalem." | Dynamic; "celebration"; adds "so" as causal connector |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "celebration" adds an interpretive nuance and "so" implies causation that is less direct in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:15 — Sentence structure and vividness in the temple cleansing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having made a whip out of cords, he drove all out of the temple, both the sheep and the oxen, and the coins of the money-changers he poured out and the tables he overturned" | Literal participle structure; preserves Greek clause order |
| LOGOS | "making a whip of cords, he drove them all out of the temple, with the sheep and oxen. And he poured out the coins of the money-changers and overturned their tables." | Formal; clear English sentences |
| PNEUMA | "So Jesus made a whip from ropes and drove them all out of the temple. He scattered the money-changers' coins across the floor and knocked over their tables." | Dynamic; "ropes" for σχοινίων; "scattered...across the floor"; "knocked over" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "scattered...across the floor" and "knocked over" add dramatic detail. "Ropes" instead of "cords" is a minor difference. "Across the floor" is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:16 — "house of trade" vs. "marketplace" for Greek οἶκον ἐμπορίου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not make the house of the Father of me a house of trade." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "do not make my Father's house a house of trade." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Stop turning my Father's house into a marketplace!" | Dynamic; "Stop turning" adds ongoing action; "marketplace" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "a house of trade." PNEUMA's "marketplace" is a valid interpretation of ἐμπόριον but "house of trade" preserves the parallel structure (house/house) in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:17 — "Zeal for your house" vs. "Passion for your house" for Greek ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The zeal of the house of you will consume me." | Literal with articles |
| LOGOS | "Zeal for your house will consume me." | Formal; standard Psalm 69:9 rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Passion for your house will consume me." | Dynamic; "Passion" instead of "Zeal" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "Zeal" is the traditional rendering of ζῆλος in this Psalm 69:9 quotation and should be preserved. "Passion" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but shifts the connotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:18 — "the Jews" response framing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Then the Ioudaioi answered and said to him" | Transliterated; dual verb |
| LOGOS | "So the Jews said to him" | Formal; simplified |
| PNEUMA | "The Jewish leaders demanded" | Dynamic; adds "leaders"; "demanded" intensifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "demanded" adds intensity and "leaders" adds interpretation not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:19-20 — "raise it up" vs. "raise it" for Greek ἐγερῶ αὐτόν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in three days I will raise it" / "you will raise it in three days?" | Without "up" |
| LOGOS | "in three days I will raise it up" / "will you raise it up in three days?" | With "up" |
| PNEUMA | "in three days I will raise it up" / "you're going to raise it in three days?" | Mixed; colloquial question form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "you're going to raise it" is colloquial.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:21 — "the temple of his body" — rendering style

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But that [one] was speaking concerning the temple of the body of him." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "But he was speaking about the temple of his body." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "But when Jesus said 'this temple,' he meant his own body." | Dynamic; explicative paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA paraphrases with interpretive expansion ("when Jesus said 'this temple,' he meant...").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:24-25 — "he knew all people" vs. "he knew human nature" for Greek αὐτὸν γινώσκειν πάντας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of him knowing all...he himself knew what was in the man" | Literal; "the man" with article |
| LOGOS | "because he knew all people...he himself knew what was in man" | Formal; "all people" / "what was in man" |
| PNEUMA | "because he knew human nature. No one needed to tell him about people — he already knew what was in their hearts." | Dynamic; "human nature"; adds "their hearts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "human nature" abstracts the Greek (which says "all" — all individuals). PNEUMA also adds "their hearts" which is not in the text (the Greek simply says "what was in man" — τί ἦν ἐν τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (rewording) | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 7 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Word choice differences (all three) | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Measurement/time conversion | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS (converted) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 2 continues the patterns established in Chapter 1. The primary axis of divergence remains EMET's literalism (transliterated names, Greek word order, preserved articles) versus PNEUMA's dynamic expansions (added words, modern equivalents, colloquial paraphrase). LOGOS consistently occupies the middle ground and is the basis for SOPHIA's consensus.

Notable in this chapter: PNEUMA's tendency to add narrative color ("demanded," "exclaimed," "scattered across the floor") and interpretive glosses ("human nature," "their hearts," "marketplace") that go beyond the Greek text. While these additions are often insightful, they cross from translation into commentary. The most significant theological divergence is PNEUMA's rendering of "hour" as "time" in verse 4, which obscures a crucial Johannine theme.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
